data.title: Four Corners Property Trust: Earn Strong Dividends From An Undervalued REIT
data.description:
Introduction & Financials

Four Corners Property Trust, Inc. (FCPT) offers a solid way to diversify away from the big names in the restaurant and retail REITs, with a strong and sustainable ~6.1% dividend, relatively low leverage, and an attractive valuation.

Four Corners Property Trust’s AFFO was solid overall, reaching $135.3 million over the first 9 months of 2025, meaning ~$1.33 per share compared to $1.29 over 9M’24, for a roughly 3.1% increase so far.

Growing the per share numbers and maintaining an excellent ~99.5% occupancy rate (as of September 30, 2025) even through all the weakness seen in the environment in recent years, is certainly impressive.

Financially, based on FCPT’s latest report, we see a solid position for a REIT, and although they lack cash on hand (only ~$6.7 million), they have plenty of assets to cover their debt significantly, with an overall manageable level of leverage.

Once again, the difference between the ~$1.54 billion equity and ~$2.54 billion market cap may seem significant, but it’s important to remember that their assets are not only recorded at the price they were acquired at (not current value), but they are also depreciated, so the equity is not as relevant for a company like this.

The debt maturity schedule remains solid, with barely any debt due in 2026 and an overall manageable spread that can be gradually refinanced, meaning they have some time to wait for interest rates to fall and aren’t forced to find capital fast.

Dividends

FCPT pays an attractive ~6.1% dividend at the moment of writing this, which is among the highest in their recent history and among the higher end of their industry. This dividend costs about $155 million per year, for a roughly 86% payout ratio based on the annualized AFFO estimated from their 9M report, which is fine given the industry and yield, but it doesn’t leave that much room for investing.

Risks & Opportunities

Ultimately, REITs are significantly exposed to the cost of external capital, especially as we’re talking about one that is rapidly developing. Over the medium term, rate cuts should be one of the company’s biggest catalysts, as they can end up lowering them alongside the long-term treasury yields, which can push the market back into better risk-adjusted rewards such as REITs.

But rate cuts will not be enough to lower debt costs, as there are other factors that the market takes into account for the longer-term treasuries that the SOFR is tied to, including expectations about the US economy, future inflation rates, growth, and so on.

Especially following the attack on Venezuela and more political uncertainty combined with the broader uncertainty from tariffs, the disagreement seen in the December Minutes (9-3 vote, with some members seeing it appropriate to pause after it), as well as worries about the Fed’s independence that made even Bank of America’s CEO sound the alarm, it’s not impossible to see higher debt costs for longer, even if rates continue being cut.

This would be the market’s way to say that it wants better yields for the new risks/uncertainty it has to take into account, potentially hurting REITs and the consumers for longer.

Four Corners continued diversifying away from Darden (the owner of Olive Garden, LongHorn Steakhouse, etc.) following the spinoff from them back in November 2015, they remain the company's largest tenant.

Yes, it’s a risk, and Four Corners is diversifying away from them, but especially in this environment, it’s arguably tougher to find opportunities in this industry, and high-quality concentration doesn’t have to be a bad thing.

Even then, if we look at the recent news available on Seeking Alpha or the company’s press releases, FCPT is among the most active REITs in terms of acquisitions we can find, with a dozen acquisitions in December 2025 alone (and already starting January with yet another one so far), reaching the milestone of $318 million spent on acquisitions in 2025 and developing their exposure to other high-quality tenants and industries such as veterinary clinics, beyond restaurants.

As a result of the high-rate environment, it shouldn’t be a surprise that the company isn’t able to grow their per share numbers, as the accretion of a 7% cap rate deal when issuing stock “costs” a ~6.1% dividend today (which they’ll likely increase in the long-term), while the most recent upsizing of their bank term loans came at a 4.6% fixed rate thanks to interest rate swaps back in January 2025, if they were to fund these acquisitions entirely through debt, they'd likely have to pay higher rates.

As such, it’s no wonder that they and many of their peers aren’t able to generate high growth, as it’s ultimately a normal limitation that’s fundamental to this asset class. I’d personally prefer it if they went for actual debt instead of issuing stock, as that could’ve been refinanced later along the way and resulted in easily quantifiable accretion over the long term.

Still, at the end of the day, they are growing and diversifying, and this can result in better credit ratings in the future (on top of a lower SOFR in the long term from macro improvements), potentially paving the way for an eventual switch to mostly funding their acquisitions through lower-rate debt. FCPT’s management also mentioned the following during their Q3 Earnings Call,

At the heart of FCPT is also a commitment to modulating our acquisition pace when the cost of capital becomes weaker as we saw last year and then ramping back up when things improve. Said another way, we believe how you raise capital and the cost of the capital is as important as what you purchase with it.

Given their medium size, if the company manages to continue expanding their high-quality portfolio with more solid deals, I believe we could even see that attract the attention of a buyer, especially if they want to gain exposure to Darden, which might otherwise be hard to get (FCPT is the landlord for roughly 22.9% of Darden’s ~2031 properties).

Plus, another visible effect of the high-rate environment is the consumer weakness that has been affecting many companies for years, as discretionary budgets and consumption were affected.

As mentioned before, there are several recent studies backing this up, including the recent one from Allianz that highlights how nearly half of Americans are facing more stress at the end of 2025 compared to the beginning of the year, citing factors such as lower income, job insecurity, and rising costs, with uncertainty around inflation still persisting into 2026.

Another opportunity for FCPT can come from the lease maturity schedule, with a lot of opportunities to negotiate better terms from 2027 to 2033 if needed/possible in that market.

As they say, 2027 is the first year of Darden spin-off lease maturities, and the relatively high 5.7x rent coverage can give them some leverage to ask for more, as we can argue that the location is a reason behind their solid profitability.

Risks Reiterated

Despite their significant acquisitions and solid diversification efforts, FCPT is a REIT at the end of the day, which exposes them significantly to external factors such as the cost of external capital, which means that they will be limited by the economy.

Especially if the stock falls a bit more, the accretion of their investments would be even smaller, potentially even getting into a meaningless or even theoretically negative level (ultimately slowing them down significantly), while rising debt costs as they have to refinance older debt combined with the still-high operator exposure will continue to be risks to keep in mind.

Valuation

Starting with FCPT’s annualized AFFO (based on the 9M 2025 level), we get to roughly $180 million in 2025, which we’ll assume is followed by a CAGR of 4% over the first 5 years (even below the market’s revenue expectations) thanks to potential to see better costs of capital delivering better spreads between investing and issuing, more activity/demand from operators to expand in an improving environment and accretive results backed by future rent escalators, followed by a 3.25% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 2.5% terminal growth rate, as well as a 9% discount rate to take into account the concentration risks, with 104.28 million shares outstanding as of the Q3 report.

This way, we get to an equity value of $3.10 billion, for an intrinsic value of $29.75, which is above the ~$24.09 seen at the moment of writing this.

Conclusions

FCPT is rated a Buy thanks to their solid financials and undervaluation, with very significant investing activity that can turn out to be rewarding in the future and a very solid and healthy dividend.

There are company-specific risks on top of the ones coming from the industry/environment, but I believe the overall risk-reward is in the investment’s favor, and it’s definitely worth considering, especially for portfolios looking for diversification away from the classic names in the industry.

data.title: Blue Owl: A Controversial High Yield Play
data.description:
Shares of alternative asset manager Blue Owl Capital Inc. (OWL) have fallen 40% since reaching an all-time high of $26.73 in January 2025 despite stellar performance from its direct lending portfolio. Acquisitions have accelerated its top line growth and non-GAAP distributable earnings, but owing to attendant dilution, per share growth has lagged, which has weighed on its stock price. With a 5.7% yield, strong fund raising, a solid direct lending portfolio, and a seemingly limitless supply of data centers to finance, the recent insider buying merited a deeper dive. An analysis follows below.

Blue Owl Capital Inc. is a New York City based alternative investment manager, boasting assets under management [AUM] of $295.6 billion, of which $183.8 billion were fee paying, as of September 30, 2025. Its major investment platforms include direct lending, real estate, and minority stakes in other alternative investment managers. With roots dating back to 2009, Blue Owl is the combination of three firms, each representing one of its primary investment platforms. Business development company Owl Rock went public in 2019, raising net proceeds of $164 million at $15.30 per share. It merged with alternative investment manager investor Dyal Capital in 2021, forming Blue Owl, which onboarded real asset manager Oak Street in 2022. Shares of OWL trade just below $16.00 a share, translating to a market cap of just under $25 billion.

The firm is capitalized by three classes of stock. The 664.8 million shares of publicly traded Class A stock confer economic interest and ‘low-vote’ privileges. The 590.1 million shares of privately held Class C stock bestow ‘low-vote’ privileges, no economic interest, and convertibility into Class A shares. The 304.7 million shares of privately held Class D stock confer no economic interest, convertibility into Class A shares, and ‘high-vote’ privileges. Owing to this complex arrangement, the principals of Blue Owl control 80% of the voting power.

Investment Platforms

Since going public, the firm has maintained an aggressive posture, not only merging with Dyal and Oak Street, but also acquiring six capital management or advisory firms since 2022 to add to its product offerings. That said, it derives the preponderance of its fee revenue from direct lending, real asset investment, and minority stakes in alternative asset management concerns. Approximately 86% of its fee revenue in the 12 months ending September 30, 2025 was generated from permanent capital, defined as products without a defined term for returning capital to investors.

Direct Lending. Over half of the firm’s AUM ($152.1 billion; $97.3 billion fee paying (PFAUM)) is invested in various credit vehicles, the largest of which is direct lending, where it provides customized financing to upper-middle-market concerns (i.e., generating revenue between $500 million and $1 billion), generally sponsored by private equity with a focus on recession-resistant industries. It has completed over 760 transactions, originating over $176 billion of debt financing across more than 150 private equity sponsors since inception (as Owl Rock). Direct lending provided fee-related revenue of $961.9 million in the first nine months of 2025 (YTD25), up 16% over YTD24, while representing 81% of its credit platform’s total fee revenue of $1.12 billion.

Real Assets. Approximately one-quarter of Blue Owl’s AUM ($74.7 billion) is primarily concentrated on acquiring net-leased commercial real estate and investing in real estate credit, both in the public and private markets. This platform is also entering digital infrastructure by acquiring, financing, and operating data centers and related assets, most notably leading financing for the three largest private credit deals in 2025, totaling $65 billion. Its $46.3 billion of FPAUM generated YTD25 fee revenue of $306.7 million.

GP Strategic Capital. The balance of Blue Owl’s AUM ($68.8 billion) is earmarked for acquiring equity stakes in, or providing debt financing to, alternative asset management firms across multiple strategies, geographies, and asset classes, collecting fixed management fees, carried interest, and a return on balance sheet investments from the underlying managers. Onboarded from the Dyal merger, its FPAUM of $40.2 billion was responsible for management fee revenue of $442.5 million in YTD25.

Other revenue, which includes administrative and transaction fees, contributed YTD25 revenue of $241.4 million across all investment platforms.

For the avoidance of doubt, AUM not earning fees is comprised of commitments from investors that have yet to be funded, capital subject to a temporary fee holiday, undeployed capital, the impact of leverage, and certain balance sheet investments.

Share Price Performance

From an operational perspective, Blue Owl has enjoyed a solid run, generating FY24 distributable earnings of $0.77 per share (non-GAAP) on total revenue of $2.30 billion, representing gains of 18% and 33% over FY23, respectively. Aided by acquisitions, revenue strength was across the board with Credit, GP Strategic Capital and Real Assets growing 37%, 13%, and 53%, respectively. That momentum has continued into YTD25, with non-GAAP distributable earnings up 14% to $926.5 million on a 27% rise in total revenue to $2.11 billion.

And in terms of new capital commitments, business remains elevated, with $57 billion added in the past 12 months, reflecting a 24% increase in AUM over that period. Blue Owl also holds $28 billion of dry powder in the form of undeployed UAM that should generate management fees of ~$360 million once invested.

That said, shares of OWL are down 40% since achieving an all-time high of $26.73 in January 2025. The answer to this conundrum is Class A share dilution from its acquisitions and [SOME] conversions from other classes of stock. Blue Owl has seen its Class A share count increase by 42% since the beginning of 2024 without a corresponding increase in its bottom line. For example, non-GAAP distributable earnings have grown 15% in the TTM ending September 30th, 2025 versus the prior year period but on a per share basis have only grown 9%.

There is also a mounting queasiness regarding the emergence of (seemingly) isolated cases of alleged fraud in the private credit markets, as well as its investments in AI – more on that below.

3Q25 Financials:

Management was adamant about the quality of its investments (in digital infrastructure and throughout its portfolio) when Blue Owl reported 3Q25 financials on October 30, 2025, posting distributable earnings of $0.22 per share (non-GAAP) on total revenue of $728.0 million versus $0.20 per share (non-GAAP) on total revenue of $600.9 million in 3Q24, representing gains of 10% and 21% (respectively), further illustrating the gains at the top line not fully translating to the per-share bottom line. That said, the firm’s fee revenue grew 23% year-over-year to $645.7 million, while also marking its 18th consecutive quarter of sequential improvement. Furthermore, the portfolio’s performance is in excellent stead with an average annualized realized loss of just 13 basis points and no signs of meaningful stress. Direct lending gross return was 13% over the prior 12 months.

Fundraising was strong with $11 billion of FPAUM raised and deployed in the quarter.

Regarding its outlook, the demand for data center financing is so vast that management could not even quantify it except to say that its debt pipeline is “in excess of $100 billion for sure” and “kind of infinite”. Of note, Blue Owl walked away from a planned $10 billion Michigan data center for Oracle (ORCL) in mid-December. Oracle stock took a hit on the news, but has since fully recovered.

The bottom-line matched expectations and the top line was $46.9 million better, but bad news from Blackrock – namely that its mid-2025 acquisition of credit giant HPS Investment Partners had already spawned a potential $430 million fraud event – put a damper on anything that Blue Owl management had to say about the quality of its portfolio. Shares of OWL were down 4% to $15.86 in the subsequent trading session.

That selloff continued when the firm announced the merger of two of its small-and-medium-sized business credit funds on November 5, 2025, halting redemptions in the smaller ($1.8 billion AUM) and private OBDC II fund until it completed a combination with publicly traded Blue Owl Capital Corporation (OBDC) fund and its $17.6 billion in AUM.

Seeing the market’s reaction to this deal that was not expected to close until 1Q26 while subjecting frozen OBDC II investors to 20% paper losses – based on the discount to NAV at which OBDC was trading – Blue Owl was compelled to cancel the deal on November 19, 2025, the day shares of OWL bottomed at a 23-month intraday low of $13.25. Notably, last week the company announced that it had significantly boosted the amount of cash (from 5% to 17%) its investors can pull from one of its private credit funds.

Balance Sheet & Analyst Commentary:

On September 30, 2025, the firm’s balance sheet reflected cash of $137.3 million and investments in its own products of $513.9 million against debt of $3.28 billion (at an after-tax cost of 3.8%) with additional liquidity of $1.64 billion. Blue Owl pays a $0.225 per share quarterly dividend for a current yield of 5.6%. Although it has only earned distributable income of $0.81 per share (non-GAAP) over the past 12 months, its dividend is safe due to the effect of non-cash depreciation, amortization, and equity-based compensation. As such, the firm generated $873.2 million in cash from operating activities in the first nine months of FY25 (YTD25) while paying out $397.2 million in dividends. Blue Owl is also authorized to repurchase up to $150 million of stock but has only bought back $1.7 million worth in YTD25.

Blue Owl bulls added one to their ranks when Raymond James upgraded the firm from a hold to a buy on December 10, 2025, citing strong fundraising, solid credit, and manageable redemption risk. The analyst community has remained largely positive on Blue Owl over the past few months (below).

On average, sell-side analysts expect Blue Owl to generate distributable earnings of $0.82 per share (non-GAAP) on revenue of $2.67 billion in FY25, followed by distributable earnings $0.98 per share (non-GAAP) on revenue of $3.18 billion in FY26.

After arguing the merits of their portfolio investments and approach on its 3Q25 conference call, management put its money where its mouth was, with the two co-CEOs, a co-president, and CFO collectively purchasing 395,670 shares of OWL around the $15 level on December 1-2, 2025.

Verdict:

They see a disconnect with the performance of its credit portfolio against negative perceptions courtesy of some high-profile episodic instances of alleged fraud in other private credit portfolios that have compelled the market to compress its multiple. Trading at 16 times FY26E non-GAAP distributable earnings with $28 billion of dry powder AUM, it can be argued that Blue Owl is cheap considering it has seemingly boundless room to grow, given that the data center construction boom will require hyperscalers to obtain $1.5 trillion of new external financing through 2030 to execute on their infrastructure plans (as per JP Morgan). Moody's projected that $3 trillion would need to be raised.

The question becomes how much capital will institutional investors and high-net-worth individuals provide to Blue Owl to make this substantial growth possible.

The potential fly-in-the-ointment is that these hyperscaler financings are asset-backed (I.e., collateralized by graphic processing units (GPU)). With newer versions of GPUs potentially hitting the market every two years, the revenue commanded by older GPU servers will decline, as will the value of the asset backing the data center financing, creating a conundrum for financiers such as Blue Owl and its backers. That said, digital infrastructure is still a small part of its entire AUM (for now) but will be the preponderance of its future growth narrative.

Add that with the Tricolor, First Brands, Broadband Telecom, and Bridgevoice cases besmirching private credit in 2025, and it becomes challenging to present a case for multiple expansion for firms like Blue Owl, irrespective of its credit portfolio’s performance. As such, I am going to stay to the sidelines on the stock.

data.title: Boeing: The Sky Is Still Clouded, Despite The Recovery Narrative
data.description:
The Boeing Company (BA) is one of the world's leading companies, crucial to the aviation market, but also to defense, two giant industries that depend heavily on Boeing.

And yet, the chart suggests that the market still has not fully priced a recovery, since before 2020, BA stock was trading at over $400, and now, even after a slight rise, the stock is still trading at $239. It is as if the company had never recovered from pre-pandemic levels. In the article I co-authored on Sungarden's profile, the stock was at $200, and Rob Isbitts found the technical setup encouraging, and that bet worked out very well.

So it's as if we've brought together an important company with several moats and the potential for an upward normalization of market cap, especially if more optimistic news comes out, such as the normalization of margins and the gradual regaining of market confidence.

The Good Side of Boeing...

There were fatal accidents, which had a very negative impact on the company and, in addition to triggering new problems, worsened existing ones, resulting in fewer deliveries, compensation payments, and very negative margins, which signals that execution really left something to be desired. But even so, the market is slowly giving the company a little more credibility again.

And indeed, there are some positive points that signal that Boeing is still a good company. In defense, for example, the company already has a positive operating margin, still under a lot of pressure, but this already signals that the worst contract harvests are now behind us, and that new ones are being made on better terms. The moat is also huge in defense. Vehicles such as the AH-64 Apache (the one in the main image of this article), of which the company still delivers a few dozen per year, is the most famous attack helicopter in the world. This is not only brand power through relationships and trust, but also has a substantial switching cost given the integration with systems, training, and the like.

In addition, there are some optionalities such as the T-7A Red Hawk, which, although still very insignificant, has newer, digital technology and should gradually replace the older fleet over time.

This also has a direct impact on service revenue, which in Q3 already reached $5.4 billion and a backlog of $25 billion, with highlights including gear repair for some U.S. Navy vehicles, as well as an agreement with Korean Air. Even though it is by far the smallest revenue stream, with all other revenue lines still under significant pressure, services already account for the largest share of operating income, as they have a much more stable margin of around 17.5%.

Commercial planes are a double-edged sword. They are currently the company's main problem, but Boeing is so big in this segment (practically a duopoly with Airbus (EADSY) or an oligopoly considering Embraer (EMBJ)) that if a normalization in margins occurs, we are talking about huge potential shareholder value to be created. In fact, the revenue per quarter for this segment is ~$11 billion, but the company ended Q3 with a backlog of $534.6 billion, and that's just for commercial airplanes.

Continuing at the same pace of deliveries/revenue, consider that if the company returns to historical margins of 8%, we are already talking about an operating income of more than $3 billion for this segment alone.

Don't Overestimate the Speed of Boeing’s Turnaround

But one of the main points we must keep in mind is that backlog does not guarantee profit. In fact, it has been one of the negative points with the company having to honor bad contracts while costs have risen. But still, let's assume that the new contracts and a good part of the backlog are now better and that the crops will occasionally be renewed. Even so, for all this backlog to turn into shareholder value, we will still have to rely on the efficient execution of strategies and initiatives, without too many management errors, as well as without too many negative surprises related to the foreign market or even chance events.

And I believe that today in the market, the main mistake may be to overestimate or perpetuate certain factors. For example, it is easy to say that all this backlog could be a huge bonus for shareholders if/when the company reaches 10% operating income, but the problem is knowing when, and in my view, this may take longer than the market expects.

Let's round up some figures to outline a baseline/slightly optimistic scenario. Assume that the pace of deliveries remains more or less the same, and by 2028 we will see Boeing operating at much more normalized levels. With $50 billion in commercial revenue and a 6% margin, we are already talking about an operating income of $3 billion.

For defense, let's assume annual revenue of $30 billion (we will consider an increase by then given geopolitical trends), and a margin of 7%, slightly higher than commercial. So we are adding another $2.1 billion in operating income.

Finally, services, with revenue of $6 billion per quarter ($24 billion annually) and a more mature margin of 20%, adding another $4 billion in operating income.

Putting it all together, we still have operating income of $9 billion, which, with the current enterprise value of $20 billion due to the high amount of debt, is a multiple of more than 23x.

The market expects revenue slightly above what the above assumptions suggest, but overall they are not outside the consensus, especially considering that while the above calculations find an EV-to-OI of 23x, market estimates find a P/E of 27x for 2028.

These estimates are rough, and I don't intend to be exact, it's just to exercise what the potential would be in a scenario where the company manages to achieve at least part of its goal. But even in this scenario, where clearly a substantial part of the factors will have to work out for Boeing to achieve these numbers, I still see a relatively high multiple.

One may argue that my estimates are too conservative, especially since in years like 2017 Boeing already achieved operating margins of 11%, and that with better momentum in defense and a better mix of revenue directed toward services, the margin could even exceed that. But note below how 2017, which delivered 11%, was an atypical year.

Putting all this together with the extremely cloudy scenario, it seems to me that Boeing's case is still not that attractive. There is enormous complexity, and even counting on normalization, the asymmetry does not seem sufficient to justify all the risks and uncertainties of the case.

The positive side I see here to justify a possible bull case is precisely the structural points that Boeing dominates several parts of very powerful industries, and this in the long term may end up unlocking substantial value that is difficult to put on paper and see when projecting a few years ahead. Perhaps in decades, this earnings yield will justify the risks, but in my view, it is a case to avoid for now.

The Bottom Line

Boeing is a very interesting thesis in the sense that it is present in promising sectors that are very important to society, but as an investment thesis, I prefer to avoid it for now and wait for both greater clarity on the turnaround results and a more obvious valuation. Therefore, my rating is a Hold.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Brighthouse Preferreds: 10.5% Yield From An Investment Grade Issuer
data.description:
Brighthouse Financial (BHF) is an investment grade rated insurance company with several series of preferred stock yielding around 10.5%. The preferreds have been hammered by delisting fears caused by the company's acquisition by a private equity firm. BHF recently released their merger proposal which shows these fears are unfounded. They stated the preferreds will continue trading on the NASDAQ with their current symbols after the merger. The market has yet to digest this information, creating an opportunity.

Background: Delisting Fears

Throughout 2025, there have been rumors that BHF would be acquired by a private equity firm. Each time those rumors surfaced, the preferred stock took another leg down on fears the preferreds would be delisted if an acquisition occurred. Below is a chart showing BHFAN's negative reaction to each incremental piece of acquisition news.

While most insurance companies that are acquired by private equity keep their preferreds listed, there was one time when an insurer was acquired by private equity and the preferreds were delisted (Enstar). In that case, like other delistings, the preferreds trade at a substantial discount due to reduced liquidity. That was the fear driving BHF preferreds prices down each time acquisition rumors surfaced.

BHF Preferreds Will Not Be Delisted

On November 6, BHF finally entered into an agreement to be acquired by private equity firm Aquarian. The initial press release and merger plan noted that the common stock would be delisted upon consummation of the merger but was silent on the preferreds.

While the preferreds initially jumped on the acquisition news, as some holders grew uneasy regarding delisting uncertainty, the preferreds sold off once again.

However, on December 23, BHF released their official Merger Proposal. This proposal explicitly states the preferreds will continue to be listed. They use the same language that similar acquisitions have used when keeping preferreds listed.

Immediately following the Effective Time, the depositary shares representing the Series A Preferred Stock, the Series B Preferred Stock, the Series C Preferred Stock and the Series D Preferred Stock will remain listed on Nasdaq under the symbols “BHFAP,” “BHFAO,” “BHFAN” and “BHFAM,” respectively, and will remain registered by Brighthouse Financial under the Exchange Act.

Source: SEC Filing

They reserve the right to delist the preferreds at some point in the future but that's not unusual. They also reserve the right to take "other actions" such as calling the preferreds. It's rare for a preferred to be delisted at a time other than immediately following the merger's close, so I am not concerned about this clause.

Parent may decide, following the Merger, to delist the depositary shares representing the Preferred Stock or the Junior Subordinated Debentures, to deregister such depositary shares or the Junior Subordinated Debentures under the Exchange Act or to take other action with respect to such depositary shares

Source: SEC Filing

Delisting Is Already More Than Priced In

Even if the BHF preferreds were delisted, their price more than reflects the discount that is typically applied to delisted securities.

A benchmark for the delisting discount are the Enstar (ESGR) preferreds. ESGR is an insurer that was acquired by an affiliate of private equity firm Sixth Street. The preferreds were delisted upon consummation of the merger in July 2025.

ESGR's fixed rate preferred now trades OTC in the expert market under the ticker ESGRF. It's trading at about $23.50 and yields 7.5%. In comparison, ESGR's Sept 2031 sr. unsecured bond yields 5.2%. So the preferreds yield 230 bps more than the medium term bonds.

We can apply a similar spread to BHF's bonds of a similar duration to see where the market would price BHF preferreds if they were delisted. BHF has a May 2030 bond that also yields 5.2%. However, since this bond has a 16 month shorter duration than the ESGR bond, it should be adjusted. Let's apply a 50 bps adjustment to account for the shorter duration which brings the yield up to 5.7%. Adding 230 bps to 5.7% indicates that if the BHF preferreds were delisted and traded at a similar spread to their bonds, then the preferreds would yield 8%.

Given the preferreds yield much more than 8%, a delisting is more than fully priced in.

Key Risk: Credit Rating Downgrades

A key risk to the thesis is if Brighthouse's creditworthiness deteriorates under new ownership. Credit rating agencies have concerns about this acquisition and have downgraded the company or put them on negative watch.

  • S&P: Brighthouse Financial 'BBB' Issuer Credit Rating Placed On CreditWatch Negative On Announced Acquisition By Aquarian
  • Moody’s Ratings places Brighthouse Financial's ratings under review for downgrade (Baa3 senior unsecured debt) following acquisition announcement
  • Fitch Downgrades Brighthouse's Issuer Rating to BBB; Outlook Stable

Source: S&P, Moody's, Fitch

Like many private equity firms that have acquired insurers, Aquarian is likely to increase risk at Brighthouse by shifting a portion of the investment portfolio from corporate bonds to CLOs. Another risk is that Aquarian is a small firm and this acquisition may be more than they can handle. Their assets under management prior to this transaction are $20B. Brighthouse has about $200B in assets so this transaction will result in a 10x increase in Aquarian's AUM.

This risk is partially offset by the deep pockets and sophistication of Aquarian's primary sources of capital -- the United Arab Emirates and Qatar. Aquarian is primarily backed by the asset management arm of the Abu Dhabi sovereign wealth fund, Mubdala Capital. Qatar's state-controlled sovereign wealth fund is a co-investor and is part of a consortium of investors that agreed to provide more than $3 billion in equity to support Aquarian's acquisition of Brighthouse.

It will be important to monitor BHF's credit ratings and any reaction in their bond prices to gauge whether risk is increasing.

Conclusion: Market Inefficiency Creates Buying Opportunity

The BHF preferreds have sold off due to fears of a delisting that are unlikely to materialize. While the initial press release and merger document were silent on whether the preferreds would be delisted, buried in the subsequent merger proposal was language stating the preferreds would remain listed. The market failed to notice this new information and tax loss selling caused further selling in the preferreds.

Below is a table of recent prices for the BHF senior securities and their yields. The best preferred to buy is the one with the highest yield. I would value the deferrable junior subordinated note BHFAL slightly more than the preferreds (~25 bps lower yield at fair value).

Ticker Price Yield
BHFAO 15.49 10.89%
BHFAP 15.25 10.82%
BHFAN 12.48 10.77%
BHFAM 11.04 10.47%
BHFAL (2058 Deferrable Jr. Bond) 16.72 9.35% (CY) 9.57% (YTM)

While most actively managed funds do not buy par $25 retail-oriented preferreds, PFFA is one fund that does. I've found the fund's manager Jay Hatfield to be an astute investor and respect his investment opinions. So, it's reassuring to see that PFFA owns a substantial position in BHF preferreds. They own 2.85M shares of various series of BHF senior securities (mainly preferreds but also a small position in the baby bond). This represents 1.84% of their portfolio.

(This is a report by Landlord Investor, one of the contributors to the Yield Hunting investment group. It was published January 2nd, 2025. All data is from that date.)

data.title: Willis Lease Finance: Shift To Asset-Light Model Should Drive Shares Higher
data.description:
I've kicked the tires on Willis Lease Finance Corporation (WLFC) a few times in the past couple of years, and have passed on it because I felt the discount to NAV in names like AerCap Holdings (AER) and Air Lease (AL) provided more compelling opportunities, despite aircraft engine leasing and service, WLFC's primary businesses, being considered to have stronger tailwinds.

This turned out to be a very good call, but times have changed. AerCap, one of my largest positions which I argued still had plenty of upside left in September, has risen 51% in the past year. Air Lease, which is being bought out, is up 33%, while WLFC has declined 25%. FTAI Aviation (FTAI) a mixed leasing and engine service business, is up 71% and trades at 35x next year's earnings. WLFC is down 25% over the same period, despite signals that it is transitioning into a more asset-light business focused on service that is in some ways similar to FTAI. Considering a starting valuation at 11x FY26 earnings and a significant discount to NAV, I believe there is asymmetric upside ahead, as two biggest things holding the shares back in my view:

  1. Capital intensity of the business.
  2. Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF) business.

Both are being changed significantly for the better in just the past few weeks.

Business Overview and FY25 Results

A brief introduction to their business, they are a "leading lessor and servicer of commercial aircraft and aircraft engines. Our principal business objective is to build value for our shareholders by acquiring commercial aircraft and engines and managing those assets in order to provide a return on investment, primarily through lease rent and maintenance reserve revenues, as well as through management fees earned for managing assets owned by other parties" per their latest 10-K.

Shares ripped higher in 2024 as the aircraft engine shortage became more understood by the market, then fell back to earth last year when the strong revenue growth failed to deliver enough to the bottom line.

Additionally, because WLFC is investing heavily in the future ($1.14 billion in purchased equipment versus just $365 million in equipment sales since 2024) there's not a lot of excess cash to repurchase shares.

This should be changing soon, as the company is pivoting towards an asset light model using capital from Blackrock credit and insurance, similar to the deal they recently signed with Liberty Mutual. Together, they provide significant capital to acquire assets into a JV structure using Blackrock and Liberty Mutual's money.

This isn't a traditional loan from Blackrock/Liberty Mutual, where the debt goes on WLFC's balance sheet. Rather, engine/aircraft assets go into an off balance sheet structure, with Blackrock providing most of the capital. Instead of Lease Rent, WLFC will be paid to manage the engine portfolio, service the engines, with some additional upside bonuses if various financial targets are hit.

The idea is that WLFC is transitioning to a more mixed, asset-light vertically integrated service business, similar to FTAI. They are expanding the service footprint with a large expansion at Teesside International Airport in the UK to support this.

WLFC is currently committed to purchasing 30 additional new LEAP-1A engines and 21 additional new LEAP-1B engines for an aggregate total of $912.6 million by 2030. These are next generation engines and in strong demand. The company recently exercised a purchase option for a deal struck in 2019, so they are buying these at below market rates.

The $912 million is purchase commitments is close to WLFC's current market cap. Rather than add debt or use OCF to pay for these engines, they can use the new partnerships to finance these engines, collect fee income, and use the cash for something else - like share repurchases or deleveraging.

Discount to NAV

Similar to AerCap, there is a huge discrepancy between Book Value and NAV, or market value of the assets. I really liked the exchange from the Q3 Conference Call.

Currently, we have what I'd estimate as a $1.4 billion NAV against a market capitalization of ~$1 billion, a significant margin of safety, valuing nothing for the actual operating business!

Expense Control/Interest Expense, including stopping the Sustainable Aviation Fuel Business

Call me old fashioned, but I don't like when companies I own invest in businesses that aren't viable without generous government subsidies. Sustainable Aviation Fuel (SAF) is one of those businesses.

Eric Gregg of Four Tree Island Advisory LLC asked the following in the Q3 Conference Call (emphasis mine.)

Then the second question is about your SAF effort. Saw the press release about Wilton International being chosen for your SAF project. As I understand that building out a 14,000-ton SAF facility would cost a few hundred million dollars. And the question is, what is Willis' intention in terms of funding that? Is it having third parties providing the bulk of that? And if that's the case, how are those conversations going?

They responded that they were looking to sell equity in the project to help fund it, but didn't give more detail. As of January 9th, they put out an 8-K saying they decided to "cease its investment in this initiative." I think this is an excellent decision and removes a big question mark around the core investment thesis with WLFC and uses for the cash it generates.

The SAF project increased expenses $9.8 million in first 9 months of the current year per their 10-Q filings. Additionally, $5.3 million is related to accelerating vesting of the shares for the prior General Counsel, plus $4.9 million in legal fees associated with "financial and strategic initiatives," likely the Liberty Mutual and Blackrock deals. Of the $43 million in increased G&A in 2025, about half looks like it should go away in 2026.

There's also good news on the interest expense front, between the two Fed cuts (should save ~$20 million/year per my math) along with them fixing a significant portion of their debt in the mid 5's.

The signs are here for operating leverage to move in the right direction in 2026.

Conclusion

WLFC has been off like a rocket so far in 2026, and I think this continues as the new business model becomes more understood.

Even after the recent strength, WLFC trades at only 11x FY26 forecast earnings, which I believe have significant upside to current estimates, and under 4x operating cash flow. If CapEx declines significantly through the use of the Blackrock and Liberty Mutual facilities, WLFC could retire a huge amount of outstanding shares at the current price.

I expect to see WLFC shares back in the $200's soon.

data.title: A 10% Cap On Credit Card Interest Rates? Just A Bad Idea
data.description:
A good idea, but only at first glance

The recent President Trump’s statements have shaken all the companies involved in the credit card business. From big banks to payment processing companies, the entire industry dropped following this news.

Please be informed that we will no longer let the American Public be “ripped off” by Credit Card Companies that are charging Interest Rates of 20 to 30%, and even more, which festered unimpeded during the Sleepy Joe Biden Administration. AFFORDABILITY! Effective January 20, 2026, I, as President of the United States, am calling for a one year cap on Credit Card Interest Rates of 10%.

President Trump on Truth Social.

The goal is setting a 10% interest rate cap over the next year in order to make life more affordable for U.S. households. Consider that the current average credit card interest rate is 19.65%, so we are talking about a significant shift.

At first glance it seems like a great idea. U.S. households would pay half of the interest on a total credit card debt of $1.06 trillion, therefore saving billions of dollars that can offer significant relief for the average U.S. household. Furthermore, an interest rate cap would be more effective for all those people with a low FICO score and forced to bear interest rates close to 30%. By the way, paying a 30% interest rate is not even the worst-case scenario.

The delinquency rate on credit card loans almost doubled (2.98%) since inflation started to be a problem once again: these people need serious support.

In light of these considerations, the 10% cap seems a necessity to lift American households from the burden of interest payments, but the harsh reality is that it won’t work. Actually, it would worsen the situation.

Why it wouldn't work

First of all, all the companies involved in the credit card business are free to set their own interest rates, even if this means 10x the Fed Funds Rate. If the government steps in and imposes a cap, this would have severe consequences on the whole industry. The interesting thing is that we know exactly what these consequences would be since the EU has already set these kinds of rules a long time ago. Every country has its own specific laws, but they tend to converge on one main principle: usury is a severe crime. And yes, 30% is most of the time classified as usury.

For example, in Italy, the Usury Threshold Rate is measured as follows: TEGM (national average rate for a specific category of loan) ×1.25 + 4%. This data is updated on a quarterly basis, and the current Usury Threshold Rate for revolving credit cards is 23.71%, not so distant from what U.S. households are used to bearing. In addition, the EU merchants tend to pay way lower transactional fees compared to US ones. In 2015, the EU regulators decided to cap the interchange fees for consumer credit card transactions at 0.30%; in the U.S., the average merchant pays an interchange fee between 2.50% and 3.50%, about 10x more.

In other words, in Europe both merchants and consumers are more protected. Merchants avoid high fees, and consumers are not overwhelmed by usurious interest rates on their credit cards. Is this a good thing? It depends.

In order to follow the EU regulation, banks and payment processing companies leave on the table a big part of their profits, something they don't do in the United States. Their profits are so thin that EU credit card issuers don’t provide any significant cashback programs. So, the first consequence of a cap on credit card interest rates would be the removal of the much-loved U.S. cashback programs.

However, let’s assume that the U.S. wants to follow the EU regulators. Is such a change possible? I don’t think so for mainly three reasons:

  • The first one is cultural. In the U.S., the credit score is everything, and using properly your credit card is one way to improve it; with a low credit score, you can’t even get a car loan, let alone a mortgage. In Europe you are not judged by your FICO score but on your income and your bank account; it doesn’t matter if you have/use your credit card or not, as long as you have a good job and some savings. Setting an interest rate cap in the U.S. would mean no cashback program and a lower incentive to buy stuff with your credit card. Consequently, a lower probability of improving your FICO score and applying for a mortgage. It is a sort of vicious/virtuous cycle that rewards people who meet deadlines and sinks those who don’t.
  • Without the cashback program, U.S. households would be less incentivized to purchase goods/services. Since 67.80% of the GDP is based on personal consumption, the removal of the cashback programs acts as a burden for the GDP growth rate.
  • For U.S. households, the credit card serves as a safety net in case of job loss. In Europe, not only is it much harder to get fired, but in most cases the government gives you a significant monthly check while you are looking for a new job. If there were a 10% cap rate in the U.S., this function provided by the banks would be available only for wealthy individuals. Nobody would lend money at 10% to an individual who has just lost his job and without collateral.

Overall, the reasons why the U.S. can’t afford an interest rate cap stem from a consumption-based culture, weak social safety nets, and a much more dynamic labor market.

Conclusion

If a 10% cap rate were the new norm, the first thing that would happen is that millions of U.S. households couldn’t rely on their credit cards anymore. If the risk of lending money remains the same but the potential gains shrink, banks would be less willing to lend money to risky individuals. Not a great move for an economy that mostly depends on private consumption.

Furthermore, people who can’t rely on banks anymore but still need money are more prone to make the huge mistake of borrowing money from unscrupulous individuals. At that point, the 30% interest rate might even look cheap.

Following the EU model wouldn’t work as well for all the social/cultural differences that I have previously mentioned. Wealthy individuals get basically free money from the cashback program, and they have no incentive to change the system as it is. If anything, the lower-middle class might push for a change, but in a world where social inequality rules, I doubt that it will happen anytime soon.

To put it simply, I believe the 10% interest rate cap has almost 0% probability of being implemented in the U.S., even if we are just talking about 12 months. Therefore, I see the recent corrections in most financial stocks as an overreaction. In particular, I am referring to Visa (V) and Mastercard (MA), two of the best companies in the world that are currently falling ~5%: I am planning to buy more shares.

data.title: NCIQ: Diversified Exposure May Not Diversify Risk
data.description:
The Hashdex Nasdaq Crypto Index US ETF (NCIQ) is a passively managed exchange-traded fund designed to provide investors with diversified exposure to the cryptocurrency market. NCIQ can be best utilized by investors seeking to invest in a diversified cryptocurrency portfolio through a standard brokerage account. Despite providing broad exposure to various cryptocurrencies, the ETF largely trades in line with Bitcoin, potentially negating the potential benefits of diversification. Looking ahead through 2026, I am expecting Bitcoin’s performance to be relatively flat as investors seek safer assets to preserve capital. As a result of the flat market expectations and the low liquidity of NCIQ, I am recommending this ETF with a Hold rating.

About Hashdex Nasdaq Crypto Index US ETF

NCIQ was launched on February 13, 2025 by Hashdex Asset Management on the Nasdaq Exchange. According to NCIQ’s November 11, 2025 prospectus update, a portion of the sponsor’s management fee will be waived through December 31, 2026, reducing the management fee from 50bps to 25bps. In terms of the management fee, the waiver places NCIQ on par with major Bitcoin ETFs like the iShares Bitcoin Trust ETF (IBIT), which has an expense ratio of 25bps.

One of the most critical factors to consider when trading high-risk strategies is liquidity. NCIQ currently has roughly $126 million in net assets, with an average of $820 thousand in share value changing hands on a daily basis. By comparison, IBIT has $70 billion in net assets and an average trading volume of $3.21 billion share value. As a result of the low liquidity, NCIQ has an average bid/ask spread of 64bps, adding substantial costs to active traders.

Looking at fund flows, NCIQ experienced -$2.23mm in net outflows at the end of December 2025, likely as a result of the price decline across the cryptocurrency market.

A major challenge the cryptocurrency market faces is the general correlation to the price of Bitcoin. Though other coins may appreciate/depreciate at different rates, the general, historical trend has been directional correlation to the price of Bitcoin, potentially negating the benefits of holding a diversified portfolio of crypto assets.

For example, comparing NCIQ to IBIT, we can see that the two price charts are nearly identical with the exception of a few months. This is primarily attributed to the nearly 75% exposure to Bitcoin in NCIQ.

Given these factors, investors and traders must weigh whether investing in a diversified portfolio is more impactful than liquidity. For active traders, it may be worth considering trading IBIT or other liquid ETFs. For long-term investors, it may be worth weighing the economic value of diversifying across other cryptocurrencies and whether it will positively impact one’s portfolio strategy. In most cases, I believe holding a pure Bitcoin ETF should suffice.

Overall, I’m somewhat neutral in terms of performance for Bitcoin for 2026. I believe the cryptocurrency may face additional selling pressure in the near-term; however, I’m expecting prices to stabilize well below prior peaks. My rationale behind this statement comes from two factors:

  • Institutionalization of Bitcoin.
  • Liquidation of leveraged Bitcoin investors.

Through the emergence of Bitcoin ETFs in January 2024, Bitcoin has largely become an institutional asset and is treated as such. This means that Bitcoin ETFs are subject to standard advisory practices such as rebalances, cash raises, and asset rotation. If the general consensus for 2026 is to rotate into less risky, value and income stocks, Bitcoin ETFs may see additional outflows in the near-term.

Roughly $19 billion in leveraged Bitcoin strategies were wiped out in October 2025 as a result of the price correction. This may mean that fewer dollars are chasing Bitcoin, potentially alleviating demand. This may result in softness in the Bitcoin market and result in additional price weakness.

Risks Related To NCIQ

NCIQ provides investors with diversified exposure in the cryptocurrency market, exposing investors to certain risks that should be considered prior to making a final investment decision. NCIQ is indexed to the Nasdaq Crypto US Settlement Price Index, a rules-based Index designed to track the performance of the cryptocurrency market. The Index is heavily weighted in Bitcoin, potentially offering little differentiation to a pure investment in Bitcoin. The price of Bitcoin has faced significant headwinds in recent months, declining from a peak of $125k/Bitcoin to roughly $81k/Bitcoin before normalizing at roughly $90k/Bitcoin. If the price of Bitcoin were to lose momentum and remain relatively rangebound, NCIQ may return minimal growth as a result of the direct exposure to Bitcoin and other cryptocurrencies.

NCIQ has limited trading volumes, potentially creating a challenging market for those seeking to actively trade the ETF. Low liquidity may add additional costs to a trader’s overall transaction costs on top of management fees.

Final Thoughts

NCIQ is a relatively risky investment strategy that provides investors with diversified exposure in the cryptocurrency market. Despite providing exposure across multiple tokens, NCIQ is heavily weighted in Bitcoin and has historically performed closely to the performance of Bitcoin. Given the low liquidity and potential normalization across the cryptocurrency market, I am recommending NCIQ with a Hold rating.

data.title: SILJ: Silver Miners Likely To Outperform In 2026
data.description:
Overview

Most of my Seeking Alpha articles are on undervalued equities, but I do occasionally write about ETFs as well when the setup is compelling enough. Presently, I think the risk reward is especially compelling for silver mining stocks, and the Amplify Junior Silver Miners ETF (SILJ) is a good option for investors that are looking for more broad-based exposure to silver miners.

This ETF is comprised of mining companies with material exposure to silver production or exploration. The minimum market cap of a company included in the ETF is $20M and the chart below highlights the 10 largest holdings in this ETF.

The stock price performance of SILJ has been very impressive over the last year, up a massive 196%, primarily due to the strong performance of silver over this period, which is up 166%. However, if we zoom out and look at the last five years, we can see that SILJ has underperformed silver by quite a bit, up “only” 110% while the price of silver is up as much as 215% over this period.

One potential reason for this underperformance is because few silver miners are pure-play silver producers and most holdings in this ETF either have gold or base metals' exposure as well, which haven't performed quite as well as silver. This might be a somewhat valid argument for base metals producers, but we can see below that gold has outperformed SILJ over the last five years as well.

Metals Vs. Mining Performance

Over a long enough period, I do think gold and silver prices have a decent chance of outperforming many mining companies due to cost inflation, equity dilution, and less than stellar management decisions. However, during a bull market, we frequently see the mining companies outperform, sometimes with a 2-3x multiple.

This is something we saw for the gold miners during the 2000s decade, and gold miners have over the last year outperformed gold rather substantially as well.

SILJ was launched in late 2012, so we have less history to look at for the ETF and silver mining companies in general. With that said, we did see the silver price jump by about 40% in the first half of 2016, following a prolonged bear market in precious metals and the miners after the 2011 peak.

Both precious metals and the precious metals miners were rather depressed at that point, which means it is far from a perfect comparison to today. However, we did see SILJ spiking up close to 300% on that 40% price increase in silver during the first half of 2016. So, I do think it is fair to assume silver miners can offer substantial leverage to the price of silver, even if there are few pure-play silver mining companies in SILJ.

Silver Price

Silver is both a precious and an industrial metal, which has, according to the Silver Institute, been in a deficit for five years now. The industrial demand is due to the conductive properties of silver and its use in solar energy and other electrical products. This demand growth doesn't look to be slowing in the near-term, even if there will always be some industrial substitution and demand destruction the higher the silver price goes.

The demand for gold is to a large degree boosted by central banks, which doesn't apply for silver to the same degree. However, investor demand outside of central banks has been relatively strong for silver over the last few years, especially in Asia, where investors have a more recent memory of currency debasements.

There is little doubt in my mind that we are now in an environment with increased geopolitical turbulence and currency debasements. There is simply no way debts can be honored in real terms, in most Western countries, where the debt to GDP is in the 300-400% range, if we include both government and private sector debt.

I don't have any predictions about the silver price in the near-term, but I do think we are likely to see higher precious metals prices for longer.

Conclusion

Silver mining companies, where SILJ is a good proxy, are priced as if silver prices will retrace substantially over the coming years. I think the silver price has a good chance of either spiking higher from here or stabilizing above $50/oz, which likely means further upside for SILJ going forward.

There is of course the risk that we either see the silver price collapse by itself or that we have more of a broad-based deflationary event, which would very likely cause the price of SILJ to drop from here. Silver mining companies are extremely volatile, both to the upside and the downside, so it is important to adjust position sizes accordingly.

I don't own SILJ directly, as I primarily look for undervalued and/or quality companies within a specific industry, but I own a handful of companies which are holdings in this ETF and I think SILJ is a good proxy for silver mining exposure. I have been increasing my exposure to silver mining companies lately, while scaling back my gold mining exposure some.

data.title: The One Chart That Signals A Major Market Shift Could Be Coming
data.description:
Ever since the beginning of 2023, mega-cap tech stocks have dominated the market's returns (SPY), while dividend stocks (SCHD) have lagged badly.

In particular, REITs (VNQ) have underperformed significantly since the beginning of 2022, and as I detailed recently, even a recent pivot to rate cuts by the Fed has failed to ignite a durable REIT rally.

While REITs continue to face material headwinds, there is one silver lining that indicates there is significant value in the space that can be had if discerning investors can find the right opportunities. That ratio is the S&P 500 (SPX) price-to-earnings multiple relative to the REIT price-to-FFO multiple. In this article, I will detail why this ratio signals that better times could be ahead for dividend investors.

The One Chart Most Income Investors Are Ignoring

At about 1.3 times today, this ratio has reached a level that historically has marked a major inflection point for income-producing real assets. In fact, this level has only been reached two other times in recorded history: the 2008–2009 Great Financial Crisis and the March 2020 COVID Shock.

Effectively, what this means is that investors are paying a 30% premium for general equity earnings relative to earnings that come from highly contracted cash flows from real asset businesses. Historically, this has reflected maximum pessimism toward rate-sensitive, income-generating real assets. It also comes as a result of several years in a row of REITs underperforming the S&P 500, and 2025 marked the widest performance gap yet between REITs and the S&P 500.

This was not driven by collapsing underlying fundamentals in many REITs. In fact, REITs in aggregate saw a 6.2% increase in FFO and a 6.3% increase in total dividends paid year-over-year through the first 3 quarters of 2025. Rather, it was driven by interest rates and significant valuation multiple expansion in the S&P 500, along with valuation multiple contraction among many REITs.

What this indicates is that this is not a sustainable trend, as valuation multiple expansion and contraction can only go on for so long, and true long-term market performance is ultimately driven by growing underlying earnings and intrinsic value.

Why Public REIT Valuations Make Little Sense

Public REITs are materially undervalued right now, as public REITs and implied cap rates have been persistently above private market valuations for the longest stretch since the early 2000s.

This makes little sense because public REITs offer many advantages that private real estate does not, namely liquidity and divisibility, which are perhaps the two biggest weaknesses for private real estate.

Additionally, they generally enjoy access to cheaper costs of capital, far greater operating efficiencies due to economies of scale, superior operating and value-add expertise, and generally better deal flow access due to experience, relationships, and exposure that the management teams provide relative to an individual investor or even a small fund that invests in private real estate.

The only real con of owning publicly traded real estate is that you are exposed to greater market volatility. However, for a long-term-oriented value investor, volatility is actually a feature rather than a bug because it enables you to buy real estate at lower prices than you would be able to get anywhere else, and then you can hold until the market prices it fairly or even ideally prices it at a premium to what it can be purchased for in the private markets.

This means that either the private markets need to see material drawdowns in valuations to where public markets are traded today, or the public markets need to re-rate. Either option gives public real estate a margin of safety relative to private market real estate, and given that the fundamentals are still quite strong in many public market REITs, such as these REITs, it would seem like, at least in some cases, it's public markets that should re-rate higher rather than private markets that should re-rate lower.

High Yields Change the Math

Not only that, but REITs and other real asset businesses like midstream infrastructure (AMLP) and utilities (XLU) all offer yields ranging between 3% and 9% at the moment, which is far greater than what the S&P 500 offers right now at just a little over 1% dividend yield.

This means that these industries need far less growth and valuation-multiple expansion to support the same returns that the S&P 500 could generate. This is especially relevant given that all three of these industries continue to grow. In particular, utilities are benefiting from AI-driven electricity demand, and midstream is benefiting from a combination of data center natural gas-driven demand, as well as re-industrialization and energy export tailwinds.

While REITs lack such dynamic tailwinds in many cases, triple-net lease REITs like Realty Income (O) still have contractual lease escalators and continue to engage in a healthy acquisition pipeline. Moreover, W. P. Carey (WPC) just delivered very strong acquisition results this past year, signaling strong growth still being had in the triple-net lease space. Thus, on top of their attractive yields, these three sectors are still growing at paces that are meeting or even beating inflation over time.

Investor Takeaway

There are plenty of REITs and other dividend-paying stocks (especially traditional consumer goods and industrial dividend growth stocks, as I recently detailed here) that are still facing material headwinds. However, I think if you can buy high-quality triple-net lease REITs on a value basis, such as O, WPC, and National Retail Properties (NNN), as well as midstream MLPs and some other high-quality utility (UTG) and infrastructure (UTF) businesses right now on a value basis, you are set to enjoy very favorable risk-adjusted returns moving forward.

This is due to their high yields that stand in the mid-to-high single digits, strong inflation-beating contractual growth rates due to inflation-linked and/or fixed annual escalators, high mandatory payouts with triple-net lease REITs having to pay out at least 90% of their taxable income each year, and midstream MLPs, utilities, and many other infrastructure businesses having long-duration fee-based contracted and/or regulated cash flows backed by solid investment-grade balance sheets.

Moreover, these sectors have healthy growth pipelines in general, with the likes of Energy Transfer (ET), Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), Enbridge (ENB), Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP), and the aforementioned triple-net lease REITs all having plenty of opportunities for acquisitions and organic growth investments. Last but not least, these sectors could certainly be re-rated higher in terms of their valuations, as their yields stand at relatively high levels compared to their own histories and look particularly attractive relative to where the S&P 500 is trading today.

As a result, these are among my very favorite places to lock in attractive and durable income, along with attractive long-term total return and attractive risk-adjusted total return potential over the long term. Therefore, these are the sorts of stocks I'm loading up on in my 7-8% yielding, low-beta, and high-returning portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Timken: Priced Already For Stability, Not For Growth
data.description:
The Timken Company (TKR) is a 100+ year old company going through a major internal reset. That, and not any particular surge in demand, has caused a localized upswing in the share price. This is an important distinction in understanding Timken now.

Timken’s revenue grew by 2.7% year over year in Q3. Adjusted EBITDA went up 50 basis points to 17.4%. Free cash flow was $164 million. This was despite a $20+ million tariff loss, and very little volume growth in some segments. That means, earnings did not improve due to demand, but due to cost discipline and pricing - part of that internal reset.

Seeing these results, management has now decided to keep on prioritizing only those business segments that can sustain ~20% EBITDA margins. Other segments will be readjusted. At the same time, they are trying to generate demand through focusing on aftermarkets, cross-sells and geographic expansions.

So the question for investors is no longer whether Timken can defend margins during stress — they can — but whether there’s further upside in earnings, and whether the recent run has already discounted any such upside.

What Timken Does

Timken is a leading manufacturer of engineered bearings and industrial motions components. These are high-end products, not sold on price alone; often, Timken works directly with customers and their engineering teams to design components that can operate under extreme load, heat, and stress. Revenue comes from the products, replacements and service.

The business is split into two segments. The first one is Engineered Bearings. This is the larger and more stable segment. EB makes various types of bearings for multiple applications. These are high-end products designed for long-term reliability, so Timken retains pricing power. Even after tariffs, EB posted positive growth in Q3, with margins near 19%.

The other segment is Industrial Motion. They make gearboxes, couplings, chains, belts, lubrication systems, and linear motion products. These products position next to bearings in the same equipment, but have higher replacement/aftermarket potential. Due to various factors - weak solar demand, solid previous year, delayed spending on maintenance - this segment is seeing lower earnings. However, its margins are actually higher than bearings in normal times.

These two segments have roughly equal sales deriving from distributors/aftermarkets and original equipment sales. Aftermarket replacement demand is less sensitive to short-term OEM dynamics. This is a key reason Timken has been able to expand margins even with modest OEM growth.

Q3 2025 Earnings Improvement

Timken’s earnings mix shift is clear from Q3 numbers. Despite revenue growing 2.7% year over year, organic growth was only 0.6%. That doesn’t sound too exciting, however, what's notable is the strong adjusted EBITDA margins and incremental margins growth I described earlier, despite tariff headwinds. That margin expansion came from three sources.

These three sources are largely directly under management control. First is pricing, which increased across both segments. Second is cost discipline, which reduced material and logistics expenses, particularly in Engineered Bearings. Third is a better mix, with higher margin applications such as wind, aerospace, and general industrial offset weaker areas like solar and agriculture.

Timken’s operating cash flow was a robust $201 million, translating to $164 million of FCF after capex. Net leverage also declined quarter over quarter to 2.1x EBITDA. This helps management continue buybacks, dividends, and targeted reinvestment without straining the balance sheet.

My key point here is that these results did not come through a sudden surge in customer demand. Demand remained soft, with some decline in Industrial Motion, actually. Yet earnings managed to move higher, showing that margins are no longer driven by short-term demand swings. Whatever Timken has done to its internal structure, it has worked.

Current Earnings Mix

Timken’s earnings mix is a little uneven. EB did the heavy lifting in Q3, having sales of $766 million. This was a 3.4% rise year over year, with margins reaching 18.8%. This high margin came through better pricing and operating performance. This was able to offset cost inflation and tariffs even without much volume growth. So we are already close to management’s long-term margin expectations, so EB provides a stable anchor to overall earnings.

IM posted slightly higher EBITDA margin, 19%, but was still lagging on near-term earnings growth. Organic sales went down 3.5% to $391 million, nearly half of EB. This was due to weaker demand from solar and agriculture and lower services revenue. Margins were maintained through pricing and cost control, but earnings were limited by lower volumes. Reported margins may well be similar, but a segment doing $766 million and growing adds far more EBITDA dollars than one doing $391 million but shrinking. This is what makes IM a drag on earnings today, and also offers the potential for upside if volume moves up.

What is important is that IM is not underperforming because it is historically low-margin. Rather, it is underperforming because of low demand from parts of the end-market. Services revenue has also not recovered, and it comes with strong margins. If these things normalize, the IM segment can contribute to incremental earnings without major capital investment.

What Management Is Actually Changing

Considering the recent margin improvement, management, led by a new CEO, is making some important changes. It is using this opportunity to reallocate capital and management focus across the portfolio.

They are using a returns-based approach to reorganize. Like I noted earlier, any business that cannot sustain ~20% EBITDA margin loses management focus, and could be downsized or exited. Those that can are going to be prioritized for investment and expansion. The shift is towards creating a portfolio based on return on capital and earnings contribution.

This shift is clearly seen in Automotives. This segment historically represented around $1 billion of annual revenue, or close to 20% of company sales. Currently, that has been reduced to about 8% of revenue, a reduction of over $600 million. Management has also indicated that around half the remaining automotive business — around 4% of total company revenue — is under active review. Automotive ties up capital but does not offer returns comparable to other segments.

At the same time, management is focusing on specific acquired platforms that already earn acceptable margins but do not have adequate geographic expansion. Within Industrial Motion, this includes Cone Drive (precision gearing for wind and automation), Rollon (linear motion for automation and robotics), and SPINEA (high-precision gearboxes). Each of these businesses serve global customers, but they still make a disproportionate share of revenue from one or two regions. So, for example, Cone Drive is skewed toward North America and Europe, Rollon is mostly Europe, and SPINEA’s revenue base is largely concentrated in Central and Eastern Europe. Management wants to use their current sales force to expand into other geographies, rather than starting new product lines.

A similar approach is happening in specialty bearings. This already generates ~20% EBITDA margin, but, owing to lower footprint rather than demand, earnings are less. Management plans to improve these areas rather than chase volume. The common point is that all these products have sound economics, the focus is now on scale. If executed well, they can support management’s focus of improving through earnings quality over revenue growth.

Timken is also undertaking a $75 million cost-saving program. They have identified overlaps in sales teams, logistics and back-office functions in IM and EB, while IM still operates with a higher cost-to-serve than EB. The focus will be to integrate these to cut costs, with $15 million targeted in Q1 2026 itself.

For investors, what matters is that these actions are internal, and quantifiable. They do not rely on a rebound in the end-market demand. So, instead of top-line growth alone, successful execution will impact segment margin growth, revenue from higher-return segments, and overall sustained FCF.

Because much of this execution is already showing up in margins and cash flow, the focus now turns to valuation and whether the recent move in the stock has already priced in that improvement.

Valuation: What the Numbers Actually Say

At its current share price (~$91), Timken is already being valued as if it can maintain its current profitability, not one where meaningful earnings expansion is still ahead.

Thus, using management’s reaffirmed FY25 adjusted EPS midpoint of $5.25, the stock trades at around 17-18x forward earnings. This is close to Timken’s own five-year average, and in line with economic peers. From an enterprise perspective, Timken trades at around 10-11x forward EBITDA, based on trailing adjusted EBITDA just under $800 million and net debt of roughly $1.7 billion. Again, that multiple is consistent with a business expected to sustain margins in the high-teens, not one that will expand beyond that level.

The free cash flow situation is solid, but already accounted for. Management expects ~$375 million of FCF in 2025, which implies an FCF yield in the mid-single digits. This will comfortably support buybacks and dividends, but does not, by itself, indicate multiple expansion. For that to happen, FCF must grow faster than earnings in this situation - which is not yet happening.

If margins do move closer to management’s target 20% EBITDA across much of the portfolio, current valuation may start appearing conservative. So, a 200-250 basis point margin lift on Timken’s current revenue base would translate to around $90-120 million of incremental EBITDA, or about $1.00-$1.30 per share. This would be an earnings change significant enough to justify higher multiples.

The problem, however, is timing and current visibility. Today’s valuation already assumes that a) margins will remain near 17–18%, b) cost savings as planned are already realized or to be delivered, c) pricing already offsets tariff, and d) IM stabilizes, not deteriorates.

What the current valuation does not assume is that IM becomes a net contributor to earnings growth, or that management’s planned portfolio reshuffle definitely lifts consolidated margins above current levels. Until one of these shows up clearly in reported numbers, the current valuation is tied to the current earnings base.

Risks And Limitations

So we see that the investment case rests on execution, not on-demand improvement. That narrows the risk set, but does not eliminate it.

Risk one is that IM may remain sluggish for longer than accounted for. Much of the potential upside depends on services revenue and maintenance activity improving. These are higher-margin segments. But if demand remains low, IM may still stabilize margins but not be able to contribute to earnings.

A second risk is that portfolio reshaping may be difficult to execute without hurting earnings. It may not be easy to exit automotive, scale acquired platforms geographically, even if necessary. In that case, earnings improvements may be delayed.

The third risk is that the current valuation leaves little margin for errors. Current price already assumes durable margins and steady cash flow. However, these things remaining just stable won’t produce upside. For upsides, they need to grow — and if they don’t, the bull case will falter.

Conclusion: Conditional Buy

Timken has already shown it can maintain stable margins during bad times. However, the stock is not cheap. Valuation has already accounted for stable margins. There are no aggressive assumptions, so this gives investors a dependable fallback option.

Upside, though, depends on improving margins that will produce higher earnings. IM also needs to contribute to growth, while EB should at least keep steady. Recent history does show this may be possible with a bit of execution. If investors believe such an execution is possible, TKR is a conditional buy.
